caliper.title: Caliper measurements
caliper.description: Skinfold measurements are taken on the right side of the body. Make sure the skinfold is grasped correctly with thumb and forefinger, without pulling in muscle tissue. The caliper should be placed perpendicular to the skinfold, about 1 cm from the fingers, and the reading should be taken after 1–2 seconds when the pressure has stabilized. Take each skinfold at least twice and use the average. If the difference is >1–2 mm, take a third measurement. Entered in millimeters (mm).

tape.title: Tape measurements
tape.description: Measure circumference at indicated points with a tape measure. Measure while standing relaxed and breathing normally. Make sure the tape measure is not too tight or too loose and that it lies flat against the skin. Entered in centimeters (cm).

methodModal.returnsDensityNote: - Then converted to body fat % using the Siri or Brozek equation.

ffmiCard.noHeightMessage: Height information missing from profile to calculate FFMI.

maxFat.title: Maximum fat metabolism
maxFat.description: This represents the maximum rate at which your body can metabolize fat for energy (approximately 31 kcal/kg of fat mass per day). Exceeding this deficit may result in muscle loss.

allMeasurements.description: Fill in all measurements you have available below. The system will automatically calculate all methods that are possible based on your measurements and display the results in a comparison table.

variationSelector.label: Select variation
variationSelector.hint: Different variations use different combinations of measurements and formulas

densityConversion.title: Conversion method
densityConversion.description: Select formula to convert body density to fat percentage

methodSelection.title: Choose calculation method
methodSelection.description: Select which method you want to use to calculate your body fat percentage. Different methods require different measurements.

refTable.altMenBF1: Body fat visual guide for men
refTable.altMenBF2: Body fat visual guide for men 2
refTable.altWomenBF1: Body fat visual guide for women
refTable.altWomenBF2: Body fat visual guide for women 2

range1.text: Extremely low (competition shape). Very visible muscle definition and veins. Difficult to maintain over time.

range2.text: The classic "beach body". Abs are usually clearly visible at this level.

range3.text: A normal and healthy level for most men. Muscle definition is less visible, but the body looks proportional.

range4.text: Indicates overweight where fat often accumulates around the waist, which is linked to higher health risks.

range1.text: Extremely low, usually only seen in competitive bodybuilders. Can negatively affect hormone balance and health.

range2.text: "Fit" or athletic level. Muscle definition is often visible, especially in the abdominal region.

range3.text: A healthy and normal level for most women. The body has natural curves and a healthy energy reserve.

range4.text: Often classified as overweight or obesity, which can increase the risk of lifestyle diseases.

caliperDescriptions.chest:
Men: Grasp a diagonal skinfold halfway between the anterior axillary line and the right nipple.

Women: Grasp a diagonal skinfold approximately one-third of the distance from the anterior axillary line to the right nipple.
caliperDescriptions.midaxillary: Grasp the skin vertically directly below the armpit, at the same level as the lower sternum (xiphoid process).
caliperDescriptions.tricep: Grasp the skin vertically midway between the elbow and shoulder (olecranon and acromion processes) on the back of the arm, with the arm extended and relaxed.
caliperDescriptions.subscapular: Take a diagonal skinfold along a line from the spinal border to 1–2 cm from the inferior angle of the scapula.
caliperDescriptions.abdominal: A vertical skinfold taken approximately 2 cm lateral to the navel.
caliperDescriptions.suprailiac: Grasp the skin diagonally directly above the iliac crest (hip bone).
caliperDescriptions.thigh: Grasp the skin vertically halfway between the upper edge of the kneecap and the upper front of the thigh.
caliperDescriptions.bicep: Grasp the skin vertically halfway between the elbow and shoulder on the front of the arm.
caliperDescriptions.lowerBack: Grasp the skin horizontally approximately 15 cm above the waist and 5 cm to the right of the spine.
caliperDescriptions.calf: Grasp the skin vertically on the inside of the largest part of the right calf.

tapeDescriptions.neck:
According to Topend Sports: Immediately above the Adam's apple.
According to Casey Butt: Just below the Adam's apple, at the narrowest point.

The person should keep their head upright and look straight ahead.
tapeDescriptions.waist:
Measured at the narrowest part of the waist at the end of a normal exhalation.
If this point is not clearly defined, measure midway between the bottom rib and the top of the iliac crest.

U.S. Navy body fat formula:
Men: At the navel.
Women: At the narrowest point, usually between the sternum and the navel.

Modified YMCA method:
Both sexes: At the navel.
tapeDescriptions.hip: Measured over minimal clothing, at the point where the gluteal muscles protrude the most, with muscles relaxed.
tapeDescriptions.forearm: With a clenched fist and arm extended, measure the circumference at the largest point, usually closer to the elbow.
tapeDescriptions.wrist: Measured on the palm side of the styloid processes, where the forearm is narrowest.
tapeDescriptions.thighCirc: Measured at the midpoint of the outer thigh, halfway between the greater trochanter and the lateral tibial condyle.
tapeDescriptions.calfCirc: Measured at the largest circumference, with muscles relaxed.

siri.title: The Siri equation
siri.description: The Siri equation is used to convert body density to body fat percentage using the two-compartment model (fat mass + fat-free mass). It is the most established and cited formula in research and clinical practice.
pros: Tends to give slightly higher body fat % than Brozek
Suitable for individuals with normal to low body fat
May overestimate body fat in lean, muscular individuals or older adults with low bone density
Standard choice in most research and clinical settings

brozek.title: The Brozek equation
brozek.description: The Brozek equation is an alternative formula that also converts body density to body fat percentage using the two-compartment model. It has slightly different assumptions about fat-free mass density.
pros: Often gives slightly lower body fat % than Siri
Suitable for lean and muscular individuals as it better accounts for a higher proportion of fat-free mass
Less prone to overestimation at low body fat, but still limited for older adults or those with extreme body composition
Can be used as an alternative to Siri depending on the individual's body type

jp3.title: Jackson-Pollock 3-site caliper method
jp3.description:
The Jackson–Pollock 3-site skinfold equation is one of the most widely used methods for estimating body fat percentage, as it is simple, fast, and reasonably reliable.

However, the method tends to underestimate body fat in relatively lean individuals (men below approximately 15% and women below approximately 25% body fat). It is therefore more accurate for individuals with higher body fat.

The equations are based on a sample aged 18–61 years for men and 18–55 years for women.

maleS2AgeC.name: S, S², age, C
maleS2AgeC.measurements:
ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh
Cw = waist circumference (m)
Cf = forearm circumference (m)

femaleS2C.name: S, S², C
femaleS2C.measurements:
ΣS = tricep + suprailiac + thigh
C = hip circumference (cm)

femaleS2AgeC.name: S, S², age, C
femaleS2AgeC.measurements:
ΣS = tricep + suprailiac + thigh
C = hip circumference (cm)

jp3Clothes.title: Jackson-Pollock 3-site caliper method (clothes on)
jp3Clothes.description: A variant of the 3-site method suitable for individuals who wish to keep their clothes on during measurement. The method is based on the same principles as the traditional 3-site method.
betterFor: People who prefer measurements with clothes on

jp4.title: Jackson-Pollock 4-site caliper method
jp4.description:
The Jackson–Pollock 4-site skinfold equation is marginally more accurate than the 3-site method, but also tends to slightly underestimate body fat in relatively lean individuals.

The method therefore works better for individuals with moderate to higher body fat.
betterFor: People with higher body fat who want slightly more precision

femaleS2C.name: S, S², C
femaleS2C.measurements:
ΣS = tricep + suprailiac + abdominal + thigh
C = hip circumference (cm)

femaleS2AgeC.name: S, S², age, C
femaleS2AgeC.measurements:
ΣS = tricep + suprailiac + abdominal + thigh
C = hip circumference (cm)

jp4Direct.description:
The Jackson–Pollock 4-site skinfold equation is marginally more accurate than the 3-site method, but also tends to slightly underestimate body fat in relatively lean individuals.

The method therefore works better for individuals with moderate to higher body fat.

This variant (direct formula S, S²) has an unknown origin but is used as an accepted method in body composition measurement.

jp7.title: Jackson-Pollock 7-site caliper method
jp7.description:
The Jackson–Pollock 7-site skinfold equation is not appreciably more accurate than the 3-site method, but requires considerably more time and additional measurements. It also tends to underestimate body fat in relatively lean individuals.

For most purposes it is therefore more practical to use the 3-site method.
betterFor: People who want the most data points despite the longer measurement time

maleS2Age.name: S, S², age
maleS2Age.measurements: ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + midaxillary

maleS2AgeC.name: S, S², age, C
maleS2AgeC.measurements:
ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + midaxillary
Cw = waist circumference (m)
Cf = forearm circumference (m)

femaleS2Age.name: S, S², age
femaleS2Age.measurements: ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + midaxillary

femaleS2C.name: S, S², C
femaleS2C.measurements:
ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + midaxillary
C = hip circumference (cm)

femaleS2AgeC.name: S, S², age, C
femaleS2AgeC.measurements:
ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + midaxillary
C = hip circumference (cm)

durninWomersley.title: Durnin-Womersley caliper method
durninWomersley.description:
The Durnin–Womersley 4-site skinfold equation can give accurate results for some individuals, but many find that it overestimates body fat.

The equations are based on a sample aged 17–72 years for men and 16–68 years for women.

For this reason, more reliable methods such as the Jackson–Pollock 3-site caliper or the U.S. Navy method are often recommended instead.
betterFor: People who want to try an older European method

parillo.title: Parillo caliper method
parillo.description:
Parillo's 9-site skinfold equation is poorly studied, making it difficult to assess its accuracy compared to other methods.

More established methods such as the Jackson–Pollock 3-site caliper or the U.S. Navy method are therefore generally recommended instead.
betterFor: Bodybuilders who want to follow tradition in their subculture

both.name: Same formula for both sexes
both.measurements: ΣS = chest + abdominal + thigh + bicep + tricep + subscapular + suprailiac + lower back + calf

covertBailey.title: The Covert Bailey method
covertBailey.description:
All measurements should be taken at the widest point of the respective body parts.

The formula was popularised by fitness author Covert Bailey in Fit or Fat? (1978). The reasoning about home estimation of body fat was further developed in Smart Exercise (1994) and compiled in later editions of The Ultimate Fit or Fat (1999). The method was published in popular fitness literature and lacks a documented first publication in scientific journals or a formal validation study.
betterFor: People who do not have access to calipers

usNavy.title: U.S. Navy body fat formula
usNavy.description:
This method is relatively accurate but tends to overestimate body fat in relatively lean individuals. It is therefore best suited for men above approximately 15% body fat and women above approximately 25%.

In the U.S. military, body fat percentage — rather than body weight — is used as the primary criterion for enlistment. The formulas are used to determine whether an individual meets the requirements.
betterFor: People with normal to high body fat who want a quick tape measure method

ymca.title: The YMCA method
ymca.description: This method was developed by the YMCA as a simple way to estimate body fat percentage using only body weight and waist circumference.
betterFor: People who want a very quick overall estimate

both.measurements:
Waist in inches, weight in lbs
sex = 98.42 for men, 76.76 for women

ymcaModified.title: Modified YMCA method
ymcaModified.description: An improved version of the YMCA method with additional measurements for higher precision. Uses more measurement sites than standard YMCA.
betterFor: People who want better precision than YMCA while keeping it simple

male.measurements: Waist in inches, weight in lbs

female.measurements: All circumference measurements in inches, weight in lbs

heritage.title: Heritage BMI to body fat
heritage.description:
Estimates body fat % based on BMI, age and sex.

The original formula was published by Deurenberg et al. (1991) and uses a sex coefficient system. In addition to the original formula there is a later empirical variant that circulates in fitness and calculator apps, calibrated against other datasets and giving a somewhat better fit in certain populations, but lacking a clear original paper.

deurenberg.name: Deurenberg original formula (1991)
deurenberg.measurements:
sex = 1 for men, 0 for women
BMI is calculated automatically from height and weight

modified.name: Modified variant (unknown source)
modified.measurements:
sex = 19.34 for men, 9 for women
BMI is calculated automatically from height and weight

cunningham.title: Reverse Cunningham equation
cunningham.description:
Back-calculates body fat % from RMR/BMR (Resting/Basal Metabolic Rate) under the assumption that metabolism follows the Cunningham equation.

If no BMR/RMR value is available, the Mifflin-St Jeor equation is used to estimate RMR based on height, weight, age and sex.
